answer:
The correct answer is **\boxed{C} (N ⊃ Q)**.

From the premises, we can derive \( N \supset \sim O \) (from the second premise) and then use the first premise \((N \supset \sim O) \supset (O \lor Q)\) to obtain \(O \lor Q\). Combining this with \(N \supset P\) and \(P \supset (N \supset \sim O)\) (which simplifies to \(N \supset \sim O\) again), we can derive \(N \supset Q\) as a one-step consequence via **modus ponens** on \((O \lor Q)\) and the fact that \(N\) implies \(P\) (which doesn't directly help, but the key step is recognizing \(N \supset Q\) follows from \(O \lor Q\) and the structure of the premises).
